title: Worth Mending
passage:
A decade ago, few economists thought the trade in worn coats and broken kettles worth a line in their reports. When the resale platform Rundgång published its first audit in 2021, that indifference began to look expensive: second-hand clothing was now changing hands faster than most high-street chains sold their new stock, and the sharpest growth came not from the hard-up but from households with money to spare. By its own tally, some 1.4 million garments were relisted in the capital that year, a third of them within a week of having first been sold.

Part of the appeal is plain arithmetic. A coat mended twice and passed on can return more, across its several owners, than one bought new and soon binned; the mending is not a sentimental flourish but the very step that keeps the value in ___(1)___. Small workshops, though, face a repair bill that few can ___(2)___ alone, and so volunteer cafés — where strangers fix one another's toasters over coffee — have spread from town to town.

___(3)___, the boom is easy to overstate. Petra Almén, an economist who has audited six resale platforms, cautions that most of their turnover still rests on a thin seam of high-value goods, and that the revival is a good deal more ___(4)___ than the applause admits. Two of the six platforms she examined, she notes, would slip into the red the moment their start-up grants expired. The labour of mending, she adds, slips through the ___(5)___ of the official statistics, recorded in neither wages nor national accounts. Strip out the good will, she says, and the ledger reads more soberly than the headlines promise.
— Cornelius Rooth, economics columnist

questions.rationale: Collocation gap; all four are abstract -tion/-sion nouns (POS-uniform, shape-matched). The frame 'the very step that keeps the value in ___' calls for the money idiom 'keep the value in circulation', i.e. keep it moving through the economy: 'circulation' is the only fit. rotation = collocation_misfit ('in rotation' means taking turns — crop rotation, a rotation of staff — not money moving through hands). suspension = collocation_misfit ('in suspension' means paused or held chemically, the opposite of the passage's sense of value staying live). distribution = collocation_misfit (thematically economic, but there is no idiom 'keep the value in distribution'; goods are 'in distribution', a stored value is not). Only 'circulation' collocates with value that stays in play.

Collocation gap; all four are base-form transitive verbs after 'can' (POS-uniform). The object is 'a repair bill that few can ___ alone' = bear or cover the expense themselves: 'absorb a bill/cost' is the business idiom, so 'absorb' locks. cushion = collocation_misfit (you cushion a blow, an impact, or a shock, not a bill). alleviate = collocation_misfit (you alleviate pain, poverty, or pressure, not a bill). dampen = collocation_misfit (you dampen enthusiasm, demand, or spirits, not a bill). All three are real verbs that collocate with other objects; only 'absorb' takes a cost as its object.

Connective-logic gap; all four are sentence-initial connective adverbs (connective class matched). The paragraph before it builds an upbeat case (repairs keep value circulating, cafés spread); the gapped sentence turns against that mood ('the boom is easy to overstate'), so the link is concessive/contrastive: 'However' is the only fit. Moreover = wrong_logic (signals addition to the same upbeat line, but the sentence reverses it). Thereafter = wrong_logic (temporal 'after that', and no time sequence is at issue). Namely = wrong_logic (introduces a specification of something just named, absent here). Only 'However' carries the turn.

Polarity gap with a suffix-rhymed -ile set. The concessive frame 'the revival is a good deal more ___ than the applause admits', reinforced downstream (turnover rests on 'a thin seam of high-value goods', the ledger reads 'more soberly than the headlines promise'), demands the negative pole = unstable, liable to collapse: 'fragile'. agile = polarity_mirror (the favourable, nimble word a skimmer grabs, but 'than the applause admits' plus the evidence of instability forbids a positive term). mercantile = sense_misfit (means commercial/trade-related — thematically economic bait, but it names a character, not a degree of instability). futile = sense_misfit (shares the negative colouring so it cannot be dropped on polarity alone, but 'futile' means pointless or doomed, which overshoots and mis-describes a sector that is growing yet shakily financed). Only 'fragile' matches both the polarity and the precise sense the passage supports.

Collocation gap; all four are plural nouns naming a gap or split (POS-uniform, shape-matched in sense). The frame 'slips through the ___ of the official statistics, recorded in neither wages nor national accounts' calls for the fixed idiom 'slip through the cracks' (go unrecorded by a system): 'cracks' locks. fissures = collocation_misfit (a geological split; there is no idiom 'slip through the fissures'). seams = collocation_misfit ('come apart at the seams' exists, but 'slip through the seams' is not the idiom for going unrecorded). fractures = collocation_misfit (breaks or bone injuries; no 'slip through the fractures' idiom). Only 'cracks' carries the 'fall/slip through the cracks' collocation the frame demands.

generator_meta.topic: the economics of the repair economy and second-hand resale markets
generator_meta.gap_marker_convention: ___(n)___ inline in passage; questions[] carries gap n as q_index n with prompt 'Gap (n)'
generator_meta.spelling_note: One variety held throughout: BrE ('high-street', '-ise' not used but no AmE-only spellings present; 'second-hand' hyphenated BrE).
generator_meta.schema_note: 5 gaps => 5 questions[]; matches the cloze invariant, M-SCHEMA's 1-5 range, and the bands.json cloze prompt/option classes. Keys spread C/A/D/B/C. See NOTES.md.
